pages.text: When 1 m 3 marble block is cut into 2 cm thick slabs, the propor- tion of ﬁne particle generation is approximately 25% of the total amount [20]. Alternative ways to reuse several types of waste materials have been developed nowadays. It is also demonstrated that the physical properties of the marble waste aggregates are within speciﬁed limits and these materials can potentially be used as aggregates in light to medium trafﬁcked asphalt pavement bin- der layers [2]. Binici et al. [8] investigated some mechanical properties of con- crete containing marble dusts and limestone dusts. The test results showed that there were clear increases of compressive strength, abrasion resistance, and sodium sulfate resistance with the increasing amount of marble dust. Furthermore, the use of marble and granite waste aggregates for production of concrete were investigated and it is determined that they could be used to im- prove the mechanical properties, workability and chemical resis- tance of the conventional concrete mixtures [8] and [9]. Waste marble powder which is limestone originated waste material also causes a serious environmental problem. The Iscehi- sar–Afyonkarahisar region has 24 marble quarries and 400 marble processing plants for sawing and polishing of marble blocks [11]. These plants generate huge amount of artiﬁcial hills and cause environmental pollution. It is targeted to use them as construc- tional materials such as bricks in this study. Therefore the environ- mental pollution problems will be reduced as a result of using waste powder. In the specimens containing marble and granite, there is a much better bonding among the additives like cement and aggregates. Furthermore, it may be said that marble and granite replacement rendered a good condensed matrix. The increased durability of concrete can be attributed to the glass content and chemical com- position of the granite. The results of one study showed that the marble and granite waste aggregates can be used to improve the mechanical properties, workability and chemical resistance of the conventional concrete mixtures [17]. Since a large demand has been placed on building material in the building industry owing to increasing population, this has caused a chronic shortage of building material. Accumulation of unmanaged wastes, especially, in the developing countries has re- sulted in an increasing environmental concern. Recycling of such wastes as building materials appears to be a viable solution not only to such pollution problem, but also to the problem of econom- ical design of buildings [3]. In this study we emphasize both how environmental burdens in relation with stone production could be reduced to a minimum identifying possible marble dust reuses and usage of marble dusts in brick industry as an additive. 2. Experimental procedure 2.1. Characterization of waste marble powders Three different waste marble powders are the raw materials used in the present study which were collected from three different quarries located in the Southwest of Turkey, Isparta and Burdur. Sampling location map of the quarries is given in Fig. 1 . Industrial brick mortar for the experimental studies was supplied by a brick company in Turkey. It was ready to pres brick mortar. Marble waste powders ob- tained from Isparta and Burdur were used as an additive in commercial brick mortar. Waste marble powders were grinded under 40 lm before the characterization studies. The characterization tests of wa

Use of waste marble powder in brick industry N. Bilgin a, H.A. Yeprem b, S. Arslan c,d,⇑, A. Bilgin e, E. Günay f, M. Mars/C223oglu b a Middle East Technical University, Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Ankara, Turkey b Yildiz Technical University, Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Istanbul, Turkey c Batman University, Department of Chemistry, Batman, Turkey d Gebze Institute of Technology, Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Gebze, Turkey e Batman University, Department of Geological Engineering, Batman, Turkey f Tubitak Marmara Research Center, Materials Department, Gebze, Istanbul, Turkey article info Article history: Received 25 June 2011 Received in revised form 25 September 2011 Accepted 2 October 2011 Available online 29 November 2011 Keywords: Waste marble powder Natural stone Brick mortar Calcite Physico-mechanical properties Environmental concerns abstract The usability of waste marble dust as an additive material in industrial brick were investigated. Marble wastes were collected from marble deposits which are located at Southwest of Turkey and industrial brick mortar was obtained from a brick company in Istanbul. Waste marble dust and brick mortar were prepared for various processes of industrial brick investigation. Waste material in different proportions was mixed with industrial brick mortar starting amount of 0 wt.% up to 80 wt.%. in 41 /C28 /C28 mm rect- angular prisms for testing of physico-mechanical properties of the samples having different marble dust composition. These prepared prisms were pressed and sintered at three different temperatures 900, 1000 and 1100 /C176C. Flexural strengths of the test samples were given at three different temperature values of 900, 1000 and 1100 /C176C. Archimedes water displacement test was conducted with different water absorp- tion percentage values at 900, 1000 and 1100 /C176C temperatures. XRD analyses have been carried out for the additives which contain mainly calcite and small amount of dolomite minerals. It was found that the amount of marble dust additive had positive effect on the physical, chemical and mechanical strength of the produced industrial brick. With increasing demands of the construction industry, bricks quality and cost become more important day by day in Turkey. In addition, the usage of marble wastes for the production of industrial bricks has signiﬁcant important role on the recycling waste marble powder in the brick production along with a great contributions to economy and ecology of the country. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction The stone-masons often use the term marble to any rock which can be easily polished [21]. Limestone, schist, travertine or even granite can be considered as marble in the business world [23]. Marble technically can be described as a metamorphic rock that consists predominantly of calcite and/or dolomite [14]. Marble powder is used in the production of bricks which has major contri- butions to the economy. Turkey, due to its location in the Alpine-Himalayan belt, has numerous marble deposits. More than 250 marble types with dif- ferent colors and patterns are being produced from these deposits. One hundred of these are well known around the world. Only in Af- yon province, disposed waste marble material is about 125,000 ton/year [11]. Natural stone powder is generally used as a raw material or reinforcement material in various areas such as building

also it is a prominent index for determination of the brick quality as the artiﬁcial marble tile. From Fig. 10 , it can be seen that the porosity values of waste mixed bricks increase with the increasing waste. One of the reasons of this result is the CaO mineral in the waste marble as it is explained above. Because CaO is very reactive and reacts with water releasing heat around and forming Ca(OH) 2. When it is dropped in water, it suddenly starts to disintegrate and does not let us perform the test. 3.3. Mechanical strength results The ﬂexural strength is the most signiﬁcant test that can be used to assure the engineering quality in the application of build- ing materials [22]. The ﬂexural strength of the material increases as hardness of the additives increases [28]. According to Yuceturk’s study, when calcite and quartz were used as an additive, compres- sive strengths were found 97 MPa and 120 MPa respectively in cul- tured marble. In order to compare the mechanical properties of industrial bricks and waste mixed bricks, it is necessary to measure the mechanical properties such as compressive strength. Fig. 11 shows the ﬂexural strength of bricks sintered at different temper- atures (900, 1000 and 1100 /C176C) with different rates of marble waste (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80 wt.%). It could be seen in Fig. 10 that the sintering temperature and waste percentage have signiﬁcant inﬂuence on the ﬂexural strength of the compositions. Porosity of the brick is increasing due to release of CO 2 gas during the calcina- tions process which results in adverse effects to ﬂexural strength. The decreases in ﬂexural strength in marble added bricks are ex- pected results since the addition of marble powders in industrial brick mortar reduced the densities and increased the porosities. In bricks, strength values usually decrease with increasing porosi- ties. However, this case may help strong isolation in building mate- rials. Some ﬂuctuations in the diagrams are the results of the difﬁculties in sample integrities especially with increasing waste additions. Fig. 4 and also the other samples show the same properties that Calcite mineral decomposes at the range of 850–900 /C176C temperature in continuous heating and gives a strong endothermic peak. Calcite, as a result of decomposition, turns into CaO in solid phase and CO 2 in gas phase. CaCO 3 minerals Calcite and Aragonite, have the same chemical composi- tion but different crystal systems. The characteristic feature of Aragonite is an endo- thermic peak at the range of 420–485 /C176C and if this occurs in DTA diagrams, it indicates the Aragonite mineral [10], but in the present study, this peak was absent and this was an indication that waste marble powders of BN1, BN2 and BN3 sam- ples do not contain Aragonite. 2.2. Characterization of industrial brick mortar XRD analysis of the commercial brick mortar is shown in Fig. 5. It was observed that the brick mortar contains mainly SiO 2 and the other minor peaks showing the sample also contains feldspar, illite, montmorillonite and kaolinite as listed in Ta- ble 2. Especially in clay minerals that consist of sheet silicates and serpentine min- erals, crystal axis lengths and angles between crystal axes are very close to each other. As a result of this, the peak are equivalent and found at approximately same places. The shortage of these similarities leads a difﬁculty on distinguishing their peaks in the X-ray diffraction diagram. For this reason, minerals belong to sheet sil- icates can be determined better with differential thermal analysis. Occurrence of exothermic and endothermic peaks with the help of heating the sample from 0– 1200 /C176C make it easier to differentiate from each others. Particle size distributions and DTA–TG diagrams of brick mortar are given in Figs. 6 and 7 respectively. 2.3. Mixing the waste marble dust with industrial brick mortar The sintering behavior, mechanical, mineralogical and physical properties of the brick compositions with added waste marble powders up to 100 wt.% (20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 and 100 wt.%) are studied. Additives and brick mortar mixtures in different proportions are given in Table 3 . Brick compositions were prepared by mixing the brick mortar and waste marble powders in a ball mill. 8 wt.% water was added to the dried brick mixture before dry pressing. Then the mixtures were taken in the molds of size 41 /C28 /C28 mm. The samples were uniaxially pressed with a Hydraulic Press under 15 MPa pressure and Fig. 8 shows the green samples before sintering. After pressing, the size and weight of the brick samples were mea- sured to calculate the shrinkage and weight losses after sintering at various temper- atures. Sintering was carried out in electrical furnace for 3 h at 900, 1000 and 1100 /C176C. The samples were heated to sintering temperature with a heating rate of 5 /C176C/min. The sintering time was 3 h then the furnace was cooled to 800 /C176C with a cooling rate of 5 /C176C/min. The samples were left to cool inside the furnace in its nat- ural cooling rate. Size and weight of samples were measured again after sintering process. The specimens were subjected to several tests to verify their mechanical, mineralogical and physical properties i.e. shrinkage, bloating, weight changes, water absorption, porosity, and bulk density. The ﬂexural strength of samples were measured with a universal testing machine in a three point bending test with a Fig. 2. XRD results of the sample BN2. Fig. 3. Particle size distribution of waste marble powder

constant cross-head speed of 15 mm min /C01. X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) techniques were used for mineralogical analysis and for micro structural examinations. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Size and weight changes after sintering process Size and weight of the samples were measured before and after sintering processes. The changes of the weight and size were calcu- lated and graphs are given inFig. 9a–d. The Fig. 9 shows the sizes and weight changes of the sample coded as BN1, BN2 and BN3 sintered at 900, 1000 and 1100 /C176C. It was observed that the increasing marble waste ratio in the mixture caused expanding of the samples. Normally clay mineral shrinks after sintering process. The organic materials burn and result in shrinkage of the material, but waste marble powder mixtures exhibit an opposite behavior especially when it contains a high proportion of waste marble powder. Despite the decrease in weight, the size increases. The main reason for the increase of the size is the occurrence of pores in the structure due to the release of CO2 during the calcinations of CaCO3. On the surface of the most specimens, there were apparent swellings due to some gases which were captured in the structure. Also lime (CaO) which formed during the calcinations is very reactive component. It reacts with any kind of material in the air or structure. CaO is an expand- able material and it can cause an increase in the sizes, too. This is a demanded property in brick manufacturing by increasing porosity, on the other hand decreases the weight of the material. Therefore, it could be used in the industrial brick mortar. At the temperature 1100 /C176C, even 100% brick mortar shows the growth in dimensions. The reason for growing of the sizes can be explained by the start of melting and the samples cannot maintain their shapes. 900 /C176Ci s the ideal temperature for the shrinkage of samples, but at this tem- perature calcination process is not completed so some CaCO 3 re- mains in the structure which is not an acceptable state and it is a disadvantage to the quality of the material. 3.2. 3.4. X-ray diffraction (XRD) test results Figs. 12–15 show the X-ray diffraction patterns of industrial brick samples mixed with different weight percentages of waste marble powder. As expected, XRD revealed that calcite and lime formed as the main crystalline phases with some amounts of gehl- enite, quartz, vollastonite, larnite, portlandite, calciumironsilicate, hersinite, cristobalite, hematite, augite, calcium silicate, illite and feldspar. Cristobalite can be seen at the temperatures between 1000 and 1100 /C176C. As a thumb rule, Ca(OH) 2 cannot exist in the structure; however, there have been some traces of Ca(OH) 2, which is thought to have resulted from the moisture contact of the sam- ple just after the sinter process. It is because of lime is a very reac- tive compound. 3.5. Scanning electron microscopy results Porosity increases with the increasing amount of marble pow- der as shown in Fig. 16. Water absorption tests also give the same results which supports the ﬁnding. However, porosity decreases because of the increasing sintering process with the increasing temperature. But marble powder shows opposite behavior under the effect of the sintering temperature due to releasing of CO 2 which leads to porous in the structure. It is seen clearly from the SEM images of samples ( Fig. 16) which is the proof of the additives role in the bricks texture. Fig. 11. X-ray diffraction patterns for 900, 1000 and 1100 /C176C (a) 20% waste marble X-ray diffraction pattern and (b) 50% waste marble X-ray diffraction pattern. Fig. 12. Flexural strength changes with waste ratio. 454 N. Bilgin et al. / Construction and Building Materials 29 (2012) 449–457

are increased, the physico-mechanical properties of bricks are in- creased remarkably. The results for this study show that 10% by weight of marble powder can be added to an industrial brick mor- tar with no sacriﬁce of technical properties of the ﬁnal product. Adding waste marble more than 10 wt.% increases the water absorption and decreases the mechanical properties. Hardness of the brick structure increases parallel to the amount of marble pow- der. CaO is very reactive compound which reacts with water and moisture by releasing heat as an exothermic reaction to the envi- ronment and forming Ca(OH) 2. If it is calcinated before the mixing process the ﬁnal products would have better properties. There is no doubt that waste marble products are great burden for marble quarry management. When it is used as an additive in industrial brick production, they will not pay any extra money for the waste materials since miners want to get rid of those mar- ble wastes which cause visual pollution around the marble depos- its. Also usage of waste marbles as an additive will do great contributions to improvement of the environment. Therefore, the cost of brick positively will be affected by the contributions of mar- ble waste as an additive. In conclusion, with increasing demands of the construction industry, bricks quality and cost become more important day by day in Turkey. This study has signiﬁcant important role on the recycling waste marble powder in the brick production along with a great contributions to economy and ecology.
